one_line_takeaway: Visual token compression does not make LVLMs safer -- it concentrates adversarial distortion onto the surviving tokens, and CAGE, a compression-aligned encoder attack that models the unknown token budget probabilistically, exposes substantially lower robustness than compression-agnostic attacks across five compression methods and three VQA datasets.
key_finding: CAGE consistently achieves lower robust accuracy (a stronger attack) than the VEAttack baseline across all five compression mechanisms, three datasets, and all token budgets. A motivating experiment (Table 1) shows the mismatch directly: on a 16-token LLaVA the full-token attack leaves 49.7% robust accuracy, whereas an attack budget aligned to K=16 drops it to 44.4%. The relative gains are largest on the text-centric TextVQA task (up to ~39% relative reduction at 64 tokens) and generally grow as compression tightens from mild to moderate. The paper also reframes compression as a 'distortion concentrator' (not a denoiser): Top-K selection preferentially keeps heavily-corrupted high-attention tokens. Both proposed defenses give only partial mitigation (an informativeness-robustness trade-off degrades clean accuracy), while a simple Top-K attention-mass detector reaches 0.94 detection accuracy at K=4 but depends on a threshold tuned to the evaluated attack.
core_contribution: The paper (CAGE, ICML 2026) is the first study of the adversarial robustness of large vision-language models (LVLMs) when they run behind a visual-token-compression bottleneck. Its own contribution is twofold: (1) an empirical diagnosis of an 'optimization-inference mismatch' -- existing encoder-based attacks optimize perturbations over the full ~576 visual-token representation, while compressed inference only sees the K surviving tokens, so standard evaluations overestimate robustness; and (2) a new attack, the Compression-AliGnEd attack (CAGE), that aligns perturbation optimization with the compression bottleneck without knowing the deployed compression method or its token budget K. CAGE combines two loss terms: Expected Feature Disruption (EFD), which models the unknown budget K_model as a random variable drawn from a prior and weights each token's cosine-distance distortion by its retention (survival) probability, and Rank Distortion Alignment (RDA), a differentiable distribution-matching loss that pushes selection/attention scores toward the most-distorted tokens so distorted evidence is more likely to be retained.
threat_model: Realistic gray-box attacker (following VEAttack / prior encoder-based attacks). The adversary has WHITE-BOX access to the vision encoder E (commonly open-sourced) but BLACK-BOX access to both the compression module C and the downstream LLM F. Critically, the compression hyperparameters -- especially the deployment token budget K_model -- are treated as unknown runtime variables that the model owner can customize. The attacker crafts an imperceptible l-infinity image perturbation (epsilon = 2/255) that is task- and question-agnostic, aiming to maximize cosine distance between clean and adversarial encoder features so downstream VQA predictions are corrupted. Motivating context is safety-critical deployment (autonomous driving, robotics, mobile agents) where compression is used for latency/energy reasons.
limitations: (1) CAGE primarily exploits attention/selection cues, so it gains less against diversity-based selection (DivPrune), which retains tokens less aligned with attention -- e.g. at 16 tokens on GQA, DivPrune keeps 34.1% robust accuracy vs ~32.4% for VisionZIP. (2) Gains plateau and can fluctuate under extremely tight budgets (16-32 tokens) because robust accuracy already approaches the non-visual 'blind' floor (e.g. VQA-v2 blind = 44.3%), leaving little headroom. (3) The attention-based detector relies on a threshold tuned to the attacks evaluated and may fail against adaptive attackers that shift attention statistics. (4) Main evaluation centers on LLaVA (Qwen2.5-VL only in appendix), and results depend on the chosen uniform budget prior [16,192] and lambda = 0.005. (5) Still an encoder-surrogate attack, not a full end-to-end white-box attack on the deployed LLM.

datasets_used: VQA-v2 (Goyal et al., 2017), 1,000 sampled question-image pairs
quantitative_results: Motivation (Table 1, compressed LLaVA): 16-token model robust accuracy 49.7% under full-token attack (Kattack=576) vs 44.4% under aligned attack (Kattack=16)
Upper bound / 576 tokens (no compression): TextVQA robust accuracy 34.7% (baseline) -> 26.5% (CAGE), a 23.6% relative reduction; VQA-v2 55.8 -> 49.4 (-11.4%); GQA 42.3 -> 39.4 (-6.9%)
Blind reference (Kmodel=0, language-prior only): GQA 21.7%, TextVQA 10.3%, VQA-v2 44.3%
Attention-mass detector on VQA-v2: best at K=4 with 0.94 accuracy, TPR 0.91, FPR 0.03, F1 0.938
Attack config: l-infinity PGD, epsilon = 2/255, step 0.5/255, T = 100 iters, lambda = 0.005, uniform prior K_model in [16,192], run on RTX 4090
baselines_compared: VEAttack (Mei et al., 2026) -- SOTA encoder-based, downstream-agnostic vision-encoder attack; primary and directly comparable baseline under identical gray-box threat model
Random token selection (defense baseline, Table 3) to isolate whether robustness gains come from added randomness/diversity
Defense D1 (robustness-aware selection) and D2 (stochastic candidate pool)
